1: Eich yukham zahav yishna hakethem hatov tishpaknah avnei qodesh bero'sh kol chutzot (How the gold has grown dim, how the pure gold has changed! The holy stones are scattered at the head of every street). Zahav (H2091, gold) and ketem (H3800, pure/fine gold, H6337 paz is used in v2) — the two Hebrew gold-words used in Lamentations 4 signal the best quality: zahav is general; ketem (H3800) and paz (H6337) denote the finest, most pure gold. The dimming of the finest gold is the opening metaphor for the degradation of what was most valuable in Jerusalem. The 'holy stones' (avnei qodesh) scattered in the streets — likely referring either to the temple stones or metaphorically to the temple personnel (priests and Levites) now reduced to wandering.
2: Benei Tzion hayeqarim hamesullaim bapaz eichah nechshavu lenvlei cheres ma'aseh yedei yotzer (The precious sons of Zion, worth their weight in fine gold — how they are regarded as earthen vessels, the work of a potter's hands!). Paz (H6337, fine gold, the purest grade) — the same word used in Psalms 19:10 and 119:127 for the highest value. The sons of Zion weighed against fine gold — the maximum human value in ANE economic terms. The contrast with clay pots (nevlei cheres, earthen vessels) is the most extreme degradation possible: from gold-equivalent to disposable pottery. The potter's vessel imagery connects to Jer 18-19 (YHWH as potter, Israel as clay) — the sovereign remaking that Lamentations depicts as the destruction now needed for the future restoration.
3: Gam taninim chalitzu shad heniqu gurevihen bat 'ammi le'akzar kaya'enim bamidbar (Even jackals offer the breast; they nurse their young; but the daughter of my people has become cruel, like the ostriches in the desert). Taninim (H8577, jackals/sea-monsters/serpents — a contested word) — here most likely jackals or sea-animals that nurse their young; even predatory/wild creatures nurse their offspring. The bat 'ammi (daughter of my people) is more cruel than the jackals: the mother-Jerusalem has abandoned her children. The ostrich (ya'en, H3282, from ya'anah, H3284, the ostrich) in the desert — the proverbially cruel mother who abandons her eggs (Job 39:14-16: she leaves her eggs in the earth and forgets that a foot may crush them). The covenant community compared unfavorably to wild animals for lack of maternal care.
4: Davaq lashon yoneq el chikko batzama 'olelim sha'alu lechem poresh ein lahem (The tongue of the nursing infant clings to the roof of its mouth for thirst; the children beg for food, but no one gives to them). The imagery of thirst and hunger focused on the most vulnerable — nursing infants and small children. Davaq lashon (tongue clings to the palate, H1692 + H3956) — the physical sensation of dehydration, the tongue dry and stuck. Sha'alu lechem (begging for bread, from sha'al, H7592, to ask/beg) — active begging by children who have no food. The children's hunger was the ultimate condemnation of the failed society: a community that lets its children starve has lost the most basic form of social covenant.
5: Ha'okelim lema'adanim nashamu bachutzo ha'emunah 'al tola'at (Those who feasted on delicacies perish in the streets; those who were brought up in purple cling to ash heaps). Ma'adanim (H4516, delicacies/delights — from 'adan, to be delicate/luxurious) — the wealthy who ate fine food. Tolaath (H8438, scarlet/worm) — the color scarlet, dyed with the crimson worm; the royal/wealthy color of luxury garments (cf. the scarlet thread of Gen 38; the scarlet in the tabernacle; the Proverbs 31 woman who clothes her household in scarlet). The reversal: those in purple and scarlet are now clinging to the dunghills of destruction. The great reversal of Lamentations (the mighty brought low) mirrors the Magnificat's pattern (Luke 1:52-53).
6: Vayigdal avon bat 'ammi mechata't Sedom hahafucha kemo rega' velo chalu vah yadayim (For the punishment of the daughter of my people has been greater than the sin of Sodom, which was overthrown in a moment, when no hands were laid on her). The comparison to Sodom — the OT's paradigm of total divine judgment — but here reversed: Jerusalem's punishment is declared greater than Sodom's. The reason given: Sodom was destroyed in a moment (rega', H7281, an instant) without human hands; Jerusalem has endured prolonged suffering. The greater punishment suggests either greater sin or the greater covenant privilege and responsibility (Luke 12:48: to whom much is given, much will be required). The covenant context makes Jerusalem's judgment heavier because the covenant relationship made the violation more serious.
7: Zaku nezireha misheleg tzachu mechalav ademu peninim sappirum gizratam (Her princes were purer than snow, whiter than milk; their bodies more ruddy than coral, their hair like sapphire). Nezireha (H5139, her Nazirites/consecrated ones — or 'princes', if nazir is taken as the title for consecrated leaders rather than specifically Nazirite vow-keepers). The four-color sequence (snow-white, milk-white, coral-red, sapphire-blue) represents the ideal human appearance in the ancient Near East — each a prized material of highest quality. The Nazirites/princes are described in the aesthetic vocabulary of the most precious natural materials. The contrast with v8 makes the before/after starkest.
8: Chashak mishachor taram lo nikru bachutzo tzafad 'uram qotzef 'al 'atzam yavas hayah kaeitz (Now their faces are blacker than soot; they are not recognized in the streets; their skin has shriveled on their bones; it has become as dry as wood). The physical transformation of the formerly beautiful to the unrecognizable — faces blackened with soot and deprivation, skin shriveled on bones. Qotzef (H6821, to shrivel/wrinkle, a hapax legomenon) and yavas (H3001, to be dry/withered) create the texture of complete dehydration and emaciation. The unrecognizable (nikru, H5234, to recognize/acknowledge) — those who had been the embodiment of beauty are now unidentifiable. The irony: those who were the most distinctive in appearance are now the most anonymous in their suffering.
9: Tovim hayu challelei cherev mechallei ra'av sheyazubu medukkarim mitvenuat sadai (Better off were the victims of the sword than the victims of hunger, who waste away, stricken by lack of the fruits of the field). The grim calculus: a quick death by the sword is preferable to the slow death of starvation. Medukkarim (H1856, pierced/stabbed, from dakar) — those stabbed by the sword die quickly; those who starve (challelei ra'av, H2491 + H7458, pierced by famine — the same word, 'chalal', being both 'pierced' and 'profaned/slain') die slowly. The covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:22-24, 53-57 specified famine as one of the most severe judgments precisely because of its gradual, agonizing character.
10: Yedei nashim rachmamaniyot bishelu yaldeihen hayu levarot lahem beshever bat 'ammi (The hands of compassionate women have boiled their own children; they became their food during the destruction of the daughter of my people). The most terrible image in Lamentations: nashim rachamaniyot (compassionate women) — the epithet deliberately highlights the contradiction. The women who are by nature (rachamim = compassion, from the womb-root rechem) the most nurturing are reduced by famine to eating their children. This fulfills the specific covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:56-57: even the most tender and delicate woman among you will begrudge her husband and her children the flesh of the children she bears, for she intends to eat them secretly. The text's citation of this specific covenant curse makes Lamentations the experiential fulfillment of Deuteronomy's warning.
11: Kilah YHWH et chamato shafach charon afo vayatzet esh beTziyon vatochal yesodoteiha (The LORD gave full vent to his wrath; he poured out his fierce anger, and he kindled a fire in Zion that consumed its foundations). Kilah (H3615, to finish/complete/bring to an end) — YHWH has completed his wrath. Shafach charon afo (poured out the burning of his nostrils/anger) — the outpouring-of-wrath vocabulary standard in prophetic judgment oracles (cf. Ezekiel 22:31; Jeremiah 6:11). The fire consuming the foundations (yesodot, H3247) — the temple foundations themselves consumed. The destruction of the foundations represents the total demolition of what was built — not just the walls and the roof but the very foundation-stones, so that nothing can be rebuilt on the same site without first removing the rubble.
12: Lo he'eminu malkhei eretz vekol yoshevei tevel ki yavo tzar ve'oyev beshaarei Yerushalayim (The kings of the earth did not believe, nor did any of the inhabitants of the world, that foe or enemy could enter the gates of Jerusalem). The invincibility-assumption — not just Israel's assumption but the international consensus: that Jerusalem, YHWH's city, was inviolable. The temple mount's natural and supernatural defenses had made Jerusalem the symbol of divine protection (Psalms 46:4-7; Lamentations 48:12-14). The shock of the Babylonian breach is expressed as worldwide disbelief: even the kings of the earth (who were not invested in YHWH's covenant) could not believe that such a city could fall. The theological reversal: the covenant promise of protection was conditional; the unconditioned assumption of inviolability was Israel's false theology.
13: Mechata'ot nevieiha avonot kohaneiha hashofchim bekirbah dam tzaddiqim (Because of the sins of her prophets and the iniquities of her priests, who shed in the midst of her the blood of the righteous). The prophets and priests — the religious leadership — are assigned primary responsibility for Jerusalem's destruction. Kohanim (priests, H3548) and nevi'im (prophets, H5030): both the guardians of the covenant and the channels of divine communication have corrupted the covenant and misled the people. The shedding of righteous blood (dam tzaddiqim) recalls the prophets' indictment in Jeremiah and Ezekiel — the specific charge of judicial murder of the innocent (cf. Jeremiah 7:6; Ezekiel 22:27). Covenant leadership's failure is the proximate cause of covenant community's destruction.
14: Na'u ivrim bachutzoim nig'alu baddam belo yukelu yig'esh billbusheihem (They wandered blind through the streets; they were so defiled with blood that no one was able to touch their garments). The priests and prophets wander ivrim (H5787, blind/blinded) — struck with the blindness that is a covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:28-29: YHWH will strike you with madness and blindness and confusion of mind; you will grope at noon as the blind grope in darkness). Defiled with blood (nig'alu baddam) — the blood of the righteous they shed now stains their own garments, making them ritually unclean and socially untouchable. The irony: the priests who were responsible for maintaining ritual purity are now the most impure.
15: Suru tame qare'u lahem suru suru al tigga'u ki nazu gam na'u amru bagoyim lo yosifu lagur (Away! Unclean! people cried at them. Away! Away! Do not touch! So they became fugitives and wanderers; people said among the nations, They shall stay with us no longer). The lepers' cry (tame, H2931, unclean) — the same warning cry that the leper must make to warn others not to approach (Leviticus 13:45: the leper must call out 'unclean, unclean'). The priests who should have pronounced clean/unclean are now themselves the objects of the unclean-warning. Their wandering among the nations (bagoyim) and being refused further residence is the Diaspora condition of covenant curse fulfilled (Deuteronomy 28:65: among those nations you shall find no respite).
16: Penei YHWH chillequm lo yosif lehavitam penei kohanim lo nasa'u veziqqenim lo chananu (The LORD himself has scattered them; he will regard them no more; no honor was shown to the priests, no favor to the elders). Pene YHWH chillequm (the face of YHWH has scattered them) — YHWH's face as the dispersing agent. The loss of the divine face is the maximum theological punishment: YHWH's face turned toward Israel meant blessing (Numbers 6:24-26); YHWH's face turned away meant abandonment. The priests receiving no honor, the elders no favor — the social inversion: the highest honor-rank in Israelite society (priests, elders) now receives no recognition. The inversion is both political (no one respects them) and divine (YHWH no longer regards them).
17: Odenu techellenah eineinu el ezratenu hevel betzipiyyatenu tzippinu el goy lo yoshi'a (Our eyes failed, ever watching vainly for help; in our watching we watched for a nation that could not save). The failure of watching — waiting for Egypt's military relief that never came. The political background: Zedekiah's alliance with Egypt and the expectation that Egypt would intervene against Babylon (cf. Jeremiah 37:5-11: Pharaoh's army that came from Egypt but then turned back). The hevel (H1892, vanity/vapor) — the same word Ecclesiastes uses for the vanity of all things: the watching for Egypt was hevel. Jeremiah had warned against trust in Egypt (Jeremiah 37:7-9); the Lamentations poet confirms that the warning was accurate. Trust in military alliance (Egypt) as an alternative to covenant faithfulness is the typical prophetic critique.
18: Tzadu tza'adennu millechet birchoboteinu qarav qitzenu male yameinu ki ba qitzenu (They dogged our steps so that we could not walk in our streets; our end drew near; our days were numbered, for our end had come). Tzadu tza'adenu (they hunted our steps, H6658 + H6806) — the enemy as hunter, pursuing with such thoroughness that normal street-life was impossible. Qatzenu male (our end was full/complete) — the fullness of the appointed end had arrived. The vocabulary of 'end' (qetz, H7093) is the apocalyptic vocabulary used in Daniel (Lamentations 9:26-27; Lamentations 11:27, 35, 45; Lamentations 12:4, 9, 13) for the appointed time of completion. The siege's end is the appointed time fulfilled — not random historical accident but the arrival of the covenantal terminus.
19: Qallu rodefeinu min nashrei shamayim 'al he-harim dalaqunu bamidbar aru lanu (Our pursuers were swifter than the eagles of the heavens; they chased us on the mountains; they lay in wait for us in the wilderness). The eagle-speed of the pursuers — the military effectiveness of the Babylonians expressed as the swiftest of birds. The eagle (nesher, H5404) appears as a Babylonian symbol in Ezekiel 17:3 (the great eagle with great wings and long pinions, coming to Lebanon) and in Habakkuk 1:8 (their horses are swifter than leopards; their horsemen fly like an eagle). The pursuit on the mountains and ambush in the wilderness — the complete coverage of every terrain, leaving no escape route.
20: Ruach appeinu meshiach YHWH nilkad beshichitotam asher amarna betzillo nichyeh vagoyim (The breath of our nostrils, the anointed of the LORD, was caught in their pits, of whom we said, Under his shadow we shall live among the nations). Meshiach YHWH (H4899, the anointed of the LORD) — the technical Messianic title, here applied to the king (Zedekiah or Jehoiachin). The king as the community's collective life — ruach appeinu (the breath of our nostrils) — the one through whose life the community breathes. Nilkad beshichitotam (caught in their pits) — the military capture of the king (Jeremiah 52:9: they captured Zedekiah in the plains of Jericho). The theological significance: the failure of the human meshiach (anointed king) to protect the community points forward to the need for a divine Messiah who will not be 'caught in their pits' but will descend into the pit and rise again (Psalms 16:10: you will not abandon my soul to Sheol, nor let your Holy One see corruption).
21: Sisi vesmachi bat Edom yoshevet be'eretz Uz gam 'alayich ta'avor cos tishkari vetitgari (Rejoice and be glad, O daughter of Edom, you who dwell in the land of Uz! But to you also the cup shall pass; you shall become drunk and strip yourself bare). The address to Edom (bat Edom, daughter of Edom) — the hostile neighboring nation that gloated over Jerusalem's destruction (Obad 11-14; Psalms 137:7: Remember, O LORD, against the Edomites the day of Jerusalem, how they said, Lay it bare, lay it bare). The bitter irony: Edom is invited to rejoice — because her turn is coming. The cup (kos, H3563) of YHWH's wrath that Jerusalem has drunk will 'pass' to Edom too. The cup-of-wrath image is the standard prophetic vocabulary for divine judgment (Jeremiah 25:15-17; Ezekiel 23:31-33; Revelation 16:19).
22: Tam avonech bat Tziyon lo yosif lehagolech chata'tech bat Edom pagad he'la al avonoteich (The punishment of your iniquity, O daughter of Zion, is complete; he will keep you in exile no longer; but your iniquity, O daughter of Edom, he will punish; he will uncover your sins). The book's closing verses offer both consolation and warning. Tam avonech (your iniquity/punishment is complete, H8552 from tamam, to be complete/finished) — the punishment has been completed; there is a limit to the judgment. The exile is not permanent. The asymmetry: Zion's punishment is complete; Edom's is just beginning. The closing verse of Lamentations is not the despair of the preceding chapters but the quiet announcement that the appointed discipline has an end — and that the hostile nations who exploited the discipline will face their own accounting.
